image.alt: Manuscript page of "Safe in their Alabaster Chambers" (Fr124) bound into Fascicle 6, ca. 1859. Houghton Library MS Am 1118.3, h11b–c.
image.credit: Courtesy Houghton Library, Harvard University. Image used under CC BY-NC-ND 3.0.

lines.text: Babbles the Bee in a stolid ear,
Pipe the Sweet Birds in ignorant cadence -

lines: {crux:verb} the meek members of the Resurrection,
Rafter of Satin and Roof of Stone —

witnesses.archive: Houghton Library · MS Am 1118.3 · h11b–c · Fascicle 6 · ca. 1859

apparatus.context: The earliest extant copy of Fr124, bound by Dickinson herself into Fascicle 6 in late 1859. The page documents her line breaks at "Alabaster" and "members" (split across lines), her indentation of even-numbered lines, and an in-manuscript variant for the word "cadence" (with "cadences" noted as alternate). It is the textual ground from which every subsequent version of the poem departs.

endorsements.reasoning: Whatever positions we take on the variants, the Fascicle 6 copy is what Dickinson chose to preserve. The textual ground.
The page is itself an argument — the broken words, the indentation, the in-MS "cadences." Reading the poem without reading the page produces a different poem.

witnesses.title: Light laughs the breeze
witnesses.subtitle: Fascicle 6 reading
witnesses.body:
Light laughs the breeze
In her Castle above them —
Babbles the Bee in a stolid ear,
Pipe the Sweet Birds in ignorant cadence —
Ah, what sagacity perished here!

apparatus.context: The second stanza as bound in Fascicle 6 in Dickinson's hand. This is also the version that the Springfield Republican printed (under the title "The Sleeping") on 1 March 1862, presumably from a now-lost copy that had passed through Sue. The stanza places natural life — breeze, bee, birds — against the silence of the entombed, ending in the sharp ironic outcry "Ah, what sagacity perished here!"

endorsements.reasoning: Selected (by the newspaper or by whomever forwarded the poem to it) for the first published appearance. The choice was editorial rather than authorial, but it constitutes the version that nineteenth-century readers actually met.
Bound in Dickinson's own hand into the fascicle. The deliberate copy, not the correspondence draft.

witnesses.title: Sue to Emily
witnesses.subtitle: H B 74b · Houghton
witnesses.body: "I am not suited, dear Emily with the second verse — It is remarkable as the chain lightening that blinds us hot nights in the Southern sky but it does not go with the ghostly shimmer of the first verse as well as the other one."

apparatus.context: Sue's surviving criticism of the "Crescent" second stanza. She admires it — calls it "remarkable as the chain lightening" — but objects that it does not match the tone of the first verse. She prefers "the other one" — the earlier "Light laughs the breeze" — on grounds of coherence with the first stanza's ghostly cold.

endorsements.reasoning: The single most important document in this poem's history outside the autographs. Without H B 74b the revision sequence is opaque.

witnesses.title: Grand go the Years
witnesses.subtitle: sent to T.W. Higginson
witnesses.aboutPhrase: the Grand Go version sent to Higginson
witnesses.body:
Grand go the Years,
In the Crescent above them —
Worlds scoop their Arcs —
And Firmaments — row —
Diadems — drop —
And Doges — surrender —
Soundless as Dots,
On a Disc of Snow.

apparatus.context: The cosmic stanza Dickinson sent to Thomas Wentworth Higginson in her first letter (April 1862) — empires falling, "Diadems" dropping, "Doges" surrendering, the whole human-political scale reduced to "Dots on a Disc of Snow." The version she chose to introduce herself to a prospective literary advisor; a strong statement of authorial preference, though one Sue had already objected to.

endorsements.reasoning: The version Dickinson chose for Higginson — the strongest single statement of authorial preference in this textual history. The cosmic scale completes what "Alabaster" begins.

apparatus.context: Dickinson's accompanying note when she sent Sue the next attempt — the "Springs — shake the Sills" version. The question is direct evidence that she was responding to Sue's objection that the Crescent stanza did not match the ghostly cold of the first. Three words; an entire revision history hangs from them.

endorsements.reasoning: Three words that document Dickinson responding directly to editorial criticism. The textual scholarship of stanza 2 depends on this fragment.

witnesses.title: Springs — shake the Sills
witnesses.subtitle: sent to Sue
witnesses.aboutPhrase: the Sills version sent to Sue
witnesses.body:
Springs — shake the Sills —
But — the Echoes — stiffen —
Hoar — is the Window —
and numb — the Door —
Tribes of Eclipse — in Tents of Marble —
Staples of Ages — have buckled — there —

apparatus.context: The "frostier" attempt. The stanza brings the temporal world back into the crypt itself: spring shakes the sills but the echoes do not stir; the window is hoar, the door numb. The structural move is closer to Sue's wish than either of the first two stanzas. Whether Sue accepted it is not recorded.

endorsements.reasoning: Emily's "Is this frostier?" is the most direct statement of authorial intent we have. She wrote it for this version, in response to Sue.
"Hoar — is the Window — / and numb — the Door —" brings the cold inside the structure. It does what the other versions only describe.

witnesses.title: Springs — shake the seals
witnesses.subtitle: further revision
witnesses.body:
Springs — shake the seals —
But the silence — stiffens —
Frosts unhook — in Northern Zones —
Icicles — crawl from polar Caverns —
Midnight in Marble —
Refutes — the Suns —

apparatus.context: A further revision in which "Sills" becomes the uncapitalised "seals" and the domestic conceit of door-and-window gives way to polar caverns and crawling icicles. The final line — "Midnight in Marble — Refutes — the Suns —" — is one of Dickinson's most uncompromising. Whether this stanza is the further refinement of the previous or an alternative to it remains an open scholarly question.

endorsements.reasoning: "Midnight in Marble — Refutes — the Suns —" is the harshest line Dickinson wrote on this material. This version finishes the poem's argument.

witnesses.title: Springfield Daily Republican
witnesses.subtitle: first publication
witnesses.body: Printed anonymously as "The Sleeping" with the Fascicle 6 stanza ("Light laughs the breeze"). Apparently from the now-lost copy to Susan Dickinson.

apparatus.context: The first time the poem reached print. The newspaper used the Fascicle 6 version of the second stanza, not the Crescent version Dickinson had been working toward. It is possible Sue forwarded the copy; Dickinson seems to have had no editorial say.

endorsements.reasoning: The autograph reads "Sleep." No witness supports "Lie." The conjecture should sit in apparatus, not in the reading text.
The Pauline echo is essential to the poem's argument. "Sleep" positions the meek inside scriptural promise; the irony depends on it.
The visual disposition of the page rewards "Sleep" — the broken "mem-/bers" line falls with the right weight only when the verb is soft.
Vendler's argument deserves the weight she gave it. "Lie" is the language of gravestone inscription — the actual material the poem is describing.
The sound case is real. "Lie the meek members" is consonantally heavier, matching the stillness the stanza describes. "Sleep" is too soft.
